title: How Trump and the NATO Summit Lend Legitimacy to Turkey’s Autocratic President | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Henri Barkey argues the July 7–8 NATO summit in Ankara hands Turkish President Erdoğan legitimacy for his increasingly authoritarian rule, enabled by a Trump administration that has removed the last U.S. checks on his behavior and is pushing arms deals — F-110 engines for Turkey's KAAN fighter and a possible F-35 readmission — despite Turkey's unresolved S-400 purchase and deepening domestic crackdown.

insights.claim: The July 7–8 NATO summit in Ankara gives Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan a stage to project himself as 'the indispensable ally' to Europe and the U.S. amid a harsh domestic crackdown on political dissent, aiming to legitimize his rule and normalize the prolongation of his tenure since 2003.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: Signals continued institutional consolidation by Erdoğan validated by Western optics — relevant to Turkey political-risk and governance assessments.
insights.locator: He intends to present himself as

insights.claim: Turkey has managed the Russia-Ukraine war by continuing to arm Ukraine and brokering an early Black Sea corridor for Ukrainian grain exports, while simultaneously trading with Russia and welcoming Russian tourists, leveraging its Montreux Convention control of the Black Sea straits to project reassurance to both sides.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: Turkey's dual-track diplomacy is a structural hedge worth monitoring for Black Sea shipping, grain-export flows, and Russia-sanctions arbitrage risk.
insights.locator: He has managed the Russia-Ukraine conflict with skill

themes.theme: Trump removes the checks on Erdoğan

insights.claim: Trump has removed the main external check on Erdoğan's one-man rule by reversing the Biden administration's critical distance and instead praising Erdoğan at every opportunity, including siding with him over long-standing U.S. allies such as the Syrian Kurdish forces.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: A US foreign-policy realignment that removes a key external constraint on Turkish domestic authoritarian consolidation — bearish signal for Turkey's rule-of-law and civil-society trajectory.
insights.locator: Trump has removed the most significant obstacle

insights.claim: Trump twice withdrew U.S. troops from the Syria-Turkey border, effectively endorsing Turkish interventions that displaced more than 180,000 Kurds, and after Hayat Tahrir al-Sham toppled Bashar al-Assad, Trump quickly embraced new Syrian leader Ahmed al-Sharaa and lifted Syria sanctions within his purview — handing Erdoğan's Ankara another regional win.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: A repeated pattern of U.S. Syria policy subordinated to Erdoğan's preferences — predictive of continued favorable treatment for Ankara-aligned actors, relevant to Syria-reconstruction and sanctions-relief exposure.
insights.locator: After the jihadist Hayat Tahrir al-Sham toppled Syria's Bashar al-Assad

insights.claim: On the eve of the summit, Trump notified Congress of intent to sell Turkey roughly eighty F-110 aircraft engines — a deal long stalled on Capitol Hill — to power Turkey's indigenous fifth-generation KAAN stealth fighter.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable defense-export event signaling acceleration of Turkey's indigenous fighter program — relevant to defense-industrial and NATO-interoperability positioning.
insights.locator: On the eve of the summit, he notified Congress

insights.claim: Trump has suggested reinstating Turkey into the F-35 program despite Turkey's unresolved possession of the Russian S-400 missile system, which cost Turkey a $1.4 billion forfeited down payment when it was evicted from the program in 2016; VP JD Vance is now examining whether Turkey 'has complied with U.S. laws,' even though the National Defense Authorization Act bars F-35 parts sales while the S-400s remain on Turkish soil.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: A legal workaround is being engineered around a statutory bar — a Turkey F-35 readmission decision would be a bellwether of executive willingness to override congressional national-security constraints, material for Lockheed Martin and NATO defense-supply-chain exposure.
insights.locator: Vice President JD Vance has suggested the administration is examining

themes.theme: Domestic repression and the summit's prizes

insights.claim: Erdoğan has jailed his most formidable political rival, Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu, on politically motivated charges, is dismantling the leading opposition party after it pulled ahead in the polls, and routinely uses the 'kayyum' practice to let the interior ministry remove elected Kurdish mayors without cause.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: Quantifies the domestic-repression backdrop against which Western validation is occurring — a governance and political-risk marker for Turkey exposure.
insights.locator: Ekrem İmamoğlu, the Istanbul mayor and his most formidable rival

insights.claim: Europeans face a predicament between fearing abandonment by an unpredictable Trump against a revanchist Vladimir Putin and discomfort relying on an increasingly authoritarian Turkey, which nonetheless fields NATO's largest European army — pushing Europe toward pragmatic, transactional security ties with Ankara despite condemning its domestic conduct.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: Explains why Europe will keep tolerating Erdoğan's crackdown — a durable dynamic underpinning continued NATO-Turkey security cooperation regardless of human-rights concerns.
insights.locator: Europeans thus face an especially thorny predicament

insights.claim: The summit hands Erdoğan two prizes: Trump's validating presence for both foreign and domestic audiences, and international legitimacy — despite hundreds of arrests ahead of the summit and NATO denying accreditation to opposition journalists at Turkey's behest.
insights.speaker: Henri J. Barkey
insights.so_what: Frames the summit's core function as a legitimation event rather than a security milestone — relevant context for tracking Turkey's democratic backsliding alongside continued NATO integration.
insights.locator: The summit will bestow two major prizes on Erdoğan
